subtopics.title: Introduction to Narration – Direct vs Indirect Speech

blocks.title: What is Narration?
blocks.content: Narration is the art of reporting someone's words. There are two ways: Direct Speech (quoting exact words) and Indirect Speech (paraphrasing the meaning).

rows.left: Reporting verb is outside the quotes.
rows.right: Reporting verb introduces the reported speech.
Example: He said that he was happy.

blocks.title: Basic Conversion Steps
steps: Step 1: Remove quotation marks and replace with 'that' (for statements).
Step 2: Change the reporting verb (said → told, etc.) if needed.
Step 3: Change the tense of the reported speech (backshift).
Step 4: Change pronouns to match the new subject.
Step 5: Change adverbs of time and place (today → that day, here → there).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Direct speech = quoting exact words with "...".
Indirect speech = paraphrasing without quotation marks.
The reporting verb is always outside the quoted part.
Three main changes: tense, pronouns, adverbs.

blocks.title: What is Backshift?
blocks.content: When the reporting verb is in the past tense, the tense of the reported speech moves one step back in time (backshift).

blocks.title: Tense Changes – Direct to Indirect
rows: He said that he was writing.
He said that he had written.
He said that he had been writing.
He said that he had written.
He said that he had been writing.
He said that he had written.
He said that he would write.
He said that he would be writing.
He said that he would have written.

blocks.title: Example 1: Present to Past
given: She said, "I work hard."
steps: Reporting verb: said (past) → backshift.
Present Indefinite → Past Indefinite (work → worked).
Pronoun change: I → she.
Add 'that'.
blocks.answer: She said that she worked hard.

blocks.title: Example 2: Past to Past Perfect
given: He said, "I wrote a letter."
steps: Reporting verb: said (past) → backshift.
Past Indefinite → Past Perfect (wrote → had written).
Pronoun change: I → he.
blocks.answer: He said that he had written a letter.

blocks.title: Trap: No backshift when reporting verb is in Present/Future.
blocks.wrong: He says that he wrote a letter.
blocks.correct: He says that he writes a letter. (If original is 'He says, "I write a letter."')
blocks.tip: Only the past reporting verb triggers the backshift.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: If reporting verb is in past, the reported speech tense shifts one step back.
Present → Past, Past → Past Perfect, Future → Future in Past (would).
Universal truths and habitual actions do NOT change tense.
If reporting verb is in present/future, no backshift occurs.

blocks.title: Pronoun Changes
blocks.content: Pronouns change to match the person of the new subject. First person (I, we) changes according to the subject; second person (you) changes according to the object; third person remains unchanged.

blocks.title: Adverb Changes – Direct to Indirect
rows: He said that he was busy then.
He said that he was free that day.
He said that he would come the next day.
He said that he had come the previous day.
He said that he lived there.
He said that those were his.
He said, "I met him 2 years ago."
He said that he had met him 2 years before.

blocks.title: Example: Comprehensive Change
given: She said to me, "I will come here tomorrow."
steps: She said to me → She told me.
I → she (first person to subject).
will come → would come.
here → there.
tomorrow → the next day.
blocks.answer: She told me that she would come there the next day.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: First person (I/we) → changes according to the subject.
Second person (you) → changes according to the object.
Adverbs of time and place change to reflect the new perspective.

blocks.title: Rule for Assertive Sentences
blocks.content: Remove quotation marks, add 'that', and make the required tense, pronoun, and adverb changes.

blocks.title: Example 1: Simple Statement
given: He said, "I am a teacher."
steps: Remove quotes → add 'that'.
I → he (subject).
am → was (backshift).
blocks.answer: He said that he was a teacher.

blocks.title: Example 2: Universal Truth
given: The teacher said, "The Earth revolves around the Sun."
steps: Universal truth → NO tense change.
Remove quotes → add 'that'.
blocks.answer: The teacher said that the Earth revolves around the Sun.

blocks.title: Trap: Changing tense of universal truths.
blocks.wrong: The teacher said that the Earth revolved around the Sun.
blocks.correct: The teacher said that the Earth revolves around the Sun.
blocks.tip: Universal truths, historical facts, and habitual actions do NOT change tense.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Apply backshift, pronoun, and adverb changes.
Universal truths do NOT change tense.

blocks.title: Rules for Interrogative Sentences
blocks.content:
• Use 'asked', 'enquired', or 'questioned'.
• Do NOT use 'that' – use 'if/whether' for Yes/No questions.
• Use the Wh-word itself for Wh-questions.
• Change the interrogative to affirmative structure.

blocks.title: Example 1: Yes/No Question
given: She said to him, "Are you coming?"
steps: She asked him if he was coming.
Said to → asked.
Are you coming → if he was coming (backshift + pronoun).
blocks.answer: She asked him if he was coming.

blocks.title: Example 2: Wh-Question
given: He said to me, "What are you doing?"
steps: He asked me what I was doing.
Said to → asked.
What are you doing → what I was doing (Wh-word retained).
blocks.answer: He asked me what I was doing.

blocks.title: Example 3: Two-Part Question
given: The teacher said, "Have you finished your homework?"
steps: The teacher asked if I had finished my homework.
Have you finished → if I had finished (backshift).
blocks.answer: The teacher asked if I had finished my homework.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Change the sentence structure from interrogative to affirmative.

blocks.title: Rules for Imperatives
blocks.content:
• Use 'told', 'ordered', 'requested', 'advised', 'suggested' based on the tone.
• Change the imperative to an infinitive ('to' + V1).
• Negative imperatives: 'not to' + V1.

blocks.title: Example 1: Command
given: The officer said to the clerk, "Do this work."
steps: The officer ordered the clerk to do that work.
Said to → ordered (based on tone).
Do → to do (infinitive).
this → that (adverb change).
blocks.answer: The officer ordered the clerk to do that work.

blocks.title: Example 2: Request
given: He said to me, "Please help me."
steps: He requested me to help him.
Said to → requested.
help → to help (infinitive).
me → him (pronoun change).
blocks.answer: He requested me to help him.

blocks.title: Example 3: Negative Command
given: The teacher said, "Don't talk in the class."
steps: The teacher ordered not to talk in the class.
Don't talk → not to talk.
If object is not mentioned, it's implied.
blocks.answer: The teacher ordered not to talk in the class.

blocks.title: Example 4: Suggestion
given: He said, "Let's go for a walk."
steps: He suggested going for a walk. OR He suggested that we go for a walk.
'Let's' → suggested + gerund or suggested that + should.
blocks.answer: He suggested going for a walk.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Use appropriate reporting verb (ordered, requested, advised, suggested).

blocks.title: Rules for Exclamatory Sentences
blocks.content:
• Use 'exclaimed' or 'exclaimed with joy/sorrow'.
• Change the exclamation to an assertive statement.
• Use 'that' to connect.

blocks.title: Example 1: Exclamation of Joy
given: He said, "What a beautiful day!" (Or: "How beautiful the day is!")
steps: He exclaimed that it was a very beautiful day.
What a beautiful day! → it was a very beautiful day.
Use 'exclaimed'.
blocks.answer: He exclaimed that it was a very beautiful day.

blocks.title: Example 2: Exclamation of Sorrow
given: She said, "Alas! I am undone."
steps: She exclaimed with sorrow that she was undone.
Alas! → exclaimed with sorrow.
I → she.
blocks.answer: She exclaimed with sorrow that she was undone.

blocks.title: Example 3: Exclamation of Surprise
given: He said, "Oh! What a big house!"
steps: He exclaimed with surprise that it was a very big house.
Oh! What a big house! → it was a very big house.
blocks.answer: He exclaimed with surprise that it was a very big house.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Exclamations become assertive statements in indirect speech.

blocks.title: Rules for Optative Sentences
blocks.content:
• Use 'wished', 'prayed', or 'blessed'.
• Change the structure to an assertive sentence with 'that'.
• The verb often becomes 'might' or 'would'.

blocks.title: Example 1: Wish
given: He said, "May you live long!"
steps: He wished that I might live long.
May you → I might (backshift).
You → I (depending on context).
blocks.answer: He wished that I might live long.

blocks.title: Example 2: Prayer/Blessing
given: The mother said, "May God bless you!"
steps: The mother prayed that God might bless me.
May God bless you → God might bless me.
blocks.answer: The mother prayed that God might bless me.

blocks.title: Example 3: Would that Wish
given: He said, "Would that I were a bird!"
steps: He wished that he were a bird.
Would that I → he wished.
blocks.answer: He wished that he were a bird.

subtopics.title: Advanced Rules – Exceptions and Special Cases

blocks.title: Advanced Narration Rules
blocks.subtitle: Exceptions, Mixed Conditions, and Reporting Verbs

blocks.title: Exception 1 – No Backshift with Present/Future Reporting Verb
blocks.content: If the reporting verb is in present or future tense, there is no backshift.

blocks.title: Example: Present Reporting Verb
given: He says, "I am fine."
steps: Reporting verb is present → no backshift.
He says that he is fine.
blocks.answer: He says that he is fine.
blocks.formula_used: No backshift with present/future reporting verb

blocks.title: Exception 2 – Reporting Verbs with Objects
blocks.content: 'Said to' changes to 'told' when the object is present. 'Said to' can also become 'asked' for questions, 'ordered' for commands, etc.

blocks.title: Example: Said to → Told
given: He said to me, "I am busy."
steps: He said to me → He told me.
I am busy → he was busy.
blocks.answer: He told me that he was busy.

blocks.title: Exception 3 – Reporting Verb Placement
blocks.content: In direct speech, the reporting verb can be before, in the middle, or after the quoted words.

blocks.title: Example: Middle Reporting Verb
given: "I am fine," he said, "and I will come tomorrow."
steps: He said that he was fine and that he would come the next day.
Combine both parts with 'and'.
blocks.answer: He said that he was fine and that he would come the next day.

blocks.title: Trap: Forgetting the 'that' for assertions.
blocks.correct: He said that he was busy.
blocks.tip: In formal English, 'that' is usually required after 'said' in indirect speech.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Multiple direct sentences are combined with 'and' or 'but'.

blocks.title: Trap 1: Forgetting the Backshift
blocks.wrong: He said that he is a doctor.
blocks.correct: He said that he was a doctor. (If original: "I am a doctor.")
blocks.tip: Check the tense of the reporting verb (past → backshift).

blocks.title: Trap 2: Wrong Pronoun Change
blocks.wrong: She said that he was busy. (When original: "I am busy.")
blocks.correct: She said that she was busy.
blocks.tip: First person (I) changes according to the subject (she).

blocks.title: Trap 3: Using 'that' for Questions
blocks.wrong: He asked that what I was doing.
blocks.correct: He asked what I was doing.
blocks.tip: Do NOT use 'that' for questions – use the Wh-word or 'if/whether'.

blocks.title: Trap 4: Changing Universal Truths
blocks.wrong: He said that the Sun rose in the East.
blocks.correct: He said that the Sun rises in the East.
blocks.tip: Universal truths, historical facts, and habitual actions do NOT change tense.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Pronoun changes must match the new subject/object.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Identify the type of sentence (assertive, interrogative, imperative, exclamatory, optative). Apply the appropriate rules.

blocks.question: He said, "I am going to the market."
options: He said that he is going to the market.
He said that he was going to the market.
He said that he went to the market.
He said that he had gone to the market.
blocks.explanation: Reporting verb is past (said) → backshift: am going → was going. He said that he was going to the market.

blocks.question: She said to me, "Do you like coffee?"
options: She asked me if I liked coffee.
She asked me do you like coffee.
She said that I like coffee.
She asked me that I liked coffee.
blocks.explanation: Question → use 'asked' + if/whether. Do you like → if I liked (backshift).

blocks.question: The teacher said, "Don't make noise in the class."
options: The teacher said not to make noise in the class.
The teacher ordered not to make noise in the class.
The teacher told that don't make noise.
The teacher asked not to make noise.
blocks.explanation: Imperative negative → ordered + not to + V1. 'Don't make' → not to make.

blocks.question: He said, "What a beautiful sunset!"
options: He exclaimed that it was a very beautiful sunset.
He said what a beautiful sunset.
He asked what a beautiful sunset.
He exclaimed what a beautiful sunset.
blocks.explanation: Exclamatory → exclaimed + that + assertive sentence. 'What a beautiful sunset!' → it was a very beautiful sunset.

blocks.question: He said to me, "I will meet you tomorrow."
options: He told me that he would meet me tomorrow.
He told me that he will meet me the next day.
He told me that he would meet me the next day.
He told me that I will meet you tomorrow.
blocks.explanation: Said to → told. I → he. Will → would. You → me. Tomorrow → the next day.

blocks.question: She said, "The Earth revolves around the Sun."
options: She said that the Earth revolved around the Sun.
She said that the Earth revolves around the Sun.
She said that the Earth is revolving around the Sun.
She said that the Earth had revolved around the Sun.
blocks.explanation: Universal truth → no tense change. 'The Earth revolves around the Sun' remains unchanged.

blocks.question: He said, "Please help me."
options: He said to help me.
He requested to help me.
He said please help me.
He requested me to help him.
blocks.explanation: Request → requested + object + to + V1. Please help me → requested me to help him.

blocks.question: She said, "May you succeed!"
options: She said that you may succeed.
She wished that I might succeed.
She prayed that you might succeed.
She said may you succeed.
blocks.explanation: Optative → wished/prayed + that + might. May you succeed → wished that I might succeed.

blocks.question: He said, "Where are you going?"
options: He asked me where I was going.
He asked where I am going.
He said where are you going.
He asked that where I was going.
blocks.explanation: Wh-question → Wh-word + affirmative structure. Where are you going? → where I was going.

blocks.question: He said, "Let's go for a picnic."
options: He said let's go for a picnic.
He suggested going for a picnic.
He suggested to go for a picnic.
He said to go for a picnic.

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Review tense changes (backshift), pronoun changes, and the special rules for questions, commands, and exclamations. Practice with varied examples.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Direct & Indirect Speech
points: Backshift: Present → Past, Past → Past Perfect, Future → would.
Adverbs: now→then, today→that day, tomorrow→the next day, here→there.
Imperatives: Use 'ordered/requested' + to + V1 (or not to + V1).
